Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

US equities registered their first quarterly loss since the third quarter of 2023. Volatility surged and risk sentiment waned as markets grappled with the implications and considerable uncertainty surrounding the Trump administration’s policies on trade, immigration, fiscal spending, tax cuts, and deregulation. Escalating tariffs and the prospect of additional levies in April worsened the outlook for inflation and economic growth, fueling fears of stagflation and recession. Against a turbulent market backdrop, technology indices fell sharply, and growth and small-cap stocks significantly underperformed their value and large-cap counterparts, respectively.

The S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500) returned -4.27% over the period. Within the Index, four out of 11 sectors declined for the quarter. Consumer discretionary and information technology were the bottom-performing sectors, while energy and healthcare were the top-performing sectors over the period.

Performance Summary

Hartford Dividend and Growth Fund (I Share) outperformed the S&P 500 Index for the quarter.

Sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock-selection process, was a driver of relative outperformance. Allocation effect was driven by our underweight to information technology and consumer discretionary and overweight to energy.

Security selection also contributed to returns. Strong selection in information technology, consumer discretionary and consumer staples was partially offset by selection in communication services, financials, and industrials.

At the issuer level, our top-two relative contributors were not owning NVIDIA and Tesla, while our top-two relative detractors were not owning Berkshire Hathaway and an overweight to FMC.

Positioning & Outlook

We anticipate the potential for heightened volatility will persist amid early indications of economic softening. We continue to believe President Trump’s policy objectives, including tariffs and lower taxes, may prove inflationary, challenging the central bank’s ability to continue lowering rates. Volatility is selectively creating attractive investment opportunities, but the range of outcomes remains wide.

Against this backdrop, we remain focused on companies with consistent pricing power, strong balance sheets, and sustainable and growing cash flows trading at attractive valuations.

At the end of the period, our largest overweights were to healthcare, utilities, and financials. We were most underweight to information technology.

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

data.title: BST: NAV Growth Supported By Fund's Efficient Strategy
data.description:
Overview

It's common knowledge that Technology companies don't typically prioritize dividends. Instead, they opt to reinvest their earnings back into the business in order to rapidly grow revenue year after year. However, BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (NYSE:BST) uniquely positions itself as a fund that can provide exposure to technology while also putting an emphasis on monthly income. I previously covered BST and issued a buy rating due to attractive valuation and consistent monthly dividends. Since the time of my last coverage, BST has released an updated annual report for 2024 which prompted me to revisit this fund and provide some updated insights.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BST's price has fallen by about 4.9%. However, most of that decline can be attributed to the uncertainty linked to President Trump's tariff policies. When including all the distributions paid to shareholders during the same time frame, BST maintains a positive total return of a little under 3%. The main appeal of BST is the exposure it provides to tech while also providing a starting dividend yield of 8.8%. Based on the recent reporting, the earnings continue to comfortably cover the distributions.

Following the price decline, BST now trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. I believe that long-term investors who are optimistic about the future of technology could benefit from accumulation here. However, the caveat here is that BST only makes sense if you want to specifically put an emphasis on income generation. While the fund has demonstrated long-term consistency and resilience, BST is likely to continue underperforming traditional equities. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategies implemented by the fund.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fund overview, BST has exposure to 279 different holdings spread throughout different sectors. For instance, Software & Services account for 32.49% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Semiconductors and Tech Hardware, accounting for 27.24% and 12.37% of assets, respectively. The combination of these three sectors makes up over 63% of the fund, so it's safe to assume that these will have the greatest impact on BST's daily price change and total return potential.

Although BST does have some exposure to sectors outside these sub-sectors of technology, it's clear that technology is the main focus here. Therefore, it's possible that BST will experience a similar amount of volatility to the Nasdaq. However, the exposure to technology is essential since BST implements an option strategy to generate additional income from its holdings and support the dividend yield.

According to the fact sheet, BST implements OTM (out-of-the-money) options against its holdings. Options are written against a range of 30% to 40% of the underlying net assets, which I believe to be balanced because it allows the fund to harness volatility and generate income, while simultaneously allowing the fund to participate in capital appreciation during bull markets. The inclusion of an option strategy means that the growth of an asset is capped at whatever the selected strike price is. So if BST has an asset priced at $100 per share, it may write an option against it with a strike price of $110 per share. Any growth above this strike price is sacrificed in exchange for the premium that can be collected.

This is why I anticipate BST to continue underperforming most of its underlying holdings. For instance, we can see that NVIDIA (NVDA) is the largest holding at 12.02% of assets. This is followed by varying exposure to other large-cap companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Broadcom (AVGO), and Meta Platforms (META) just to name a few. The option strategy also allows BST to extract income from equities that don't traditionally pay a dividend, such as Amazon (AMZN).

Just to demonstrate the weakness of the strategy, I pulled a quick total return comparison of BST against its assets over the last three years. We can see that BST is outpaced by each of its top equities by a wide margin. So if you are an investor who seeks to maximize your total return, you would be better off maintaining a position in the underlying holdings instead.

Updated Valuation

Looking back over the last ten years, we can see that BST frequently traded at a premium or very slight discount to its NAV. For instance, BST traded at an average discount to NAV of 1.68% over the last three years. However, BST currently trades at a discount to NAV of 3.7%, making this an attractive time for accumulation. The fund demonstrated resilience during bear markets, while simultaneously proving that it can capitalize on more favorable market conditions. I am optimistic about the future of technology, so I believe that BST could be a great fund for those wanting exposure while putting an emphasis on income generation.

Since the time of my last coverage, BST released an updated annual report for the full year of 2024. Net investment income reported a loss of $0.28 per share, while net realized gains amounted to $8.14 per share. Looking back on prior years, BST has consistently generated losses in net investment income, and I would like to see this drastically improved in the future.

The poor net investment income leaves BST reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and can certainly be a weakness during market declines. For instance, the fund experienced massive losses in 2022, which BST still hasn't fully recovered from. At least the NAV has shifted to some positive growth from its lows. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $51.94 per share
  • 2021: $52.40 per share
  • 2022: $29.11 per share
  • 2023: $34.74 per share
  • 2024: $39.60 per share

The fund has still managed to increase its net assets over time, reinforcing that the strategy is efficient. For instance, net assets were $1.29B back in 2020, but has now increased to $1.38B at the end of 2024. As long as tech continues to thrive, I believe BST's assets will continue to increase.

Outlook & Risk

The most anticipated source of growth in the Technology sector is the growth of AI. So many of BST's holdings are pursuing different AI innovations, products, or services they can push to consumers. Some of the most notable companies that have AI projects in development include NVDA's GPUs, META's AI language model, or MSFT's involvement with ChatGPT. Despite BST's emphasis on income, the fund is still capable of participating in the growth of the market.

Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. BST has proven that it can partake in the growth of the tech market and since options are only written against less than half of the portfolio, the fund should be able to see some attractive capital appreciation over time.

However, there are specific risks that investors must be willing to expose themselves to. The emphasis on income means that BST will continue to pay out large distributions during years when the market is declining. As we've established, the fund is reliant on net realized gains to fuel growth and support the distribution. As you can imagine, it's difficult to generate positive net realized gains when markets are in decline, so BST has historically depended on return of capital to make up for the underperformance. Return of capital distributions get pulled directly from the NAV, which contributes to a more dramatic downside.

Just to demonstrate this, I pulled a comparison of BST against a more traditional ETF, Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), throughout the year of 2022. Market indices declined throughout 2022 due to the Fed aggressively hiking interest rates to their decade highs in an effort to combat record-breaking inflationary data. QQQ had a total loss of 32.58% for the year, while BST reported a much bigger loss of 38.29% despite having a much larger dividend yield.

Although I am optimistic about the future of the technology sector and BST's ability to participate, there are also some vulnerabilities that can slow growth. Trump did exempt tech from reciprocal tariffs, but there is growing competition in the AI market from outside the US. For instance, we already witnessed how markets negatively reacted when DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) achieved similar results to ChatGPT at a fraction of the cost. Markets declined as it caused investors to question the value of all the capital that the US has been pouring into the research of AI. Disruption from competitors could cause US confidence to slow and negatively impact BST's ability to provide positive returns.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.25 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.8%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $3.00 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, BST has had no issues out earning this amount from its underlying strategy. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $21.54 per share
  • 2021: $5.41 per share
  • 2022: ($20.29) per share
  • 2023: $8.63 per share
  • 2024: $7.86 per share

From this perspective, I am confident that the distributions will remain intact for the remainder of 2025 and beyond. Looking back over the last decade, we can see that the distribution has remained fairly consistent. Despite BST offering a high starting yield, the fund has also been able to provide some small dividend raises to shareholders. While I have no expectation of raises going forward, it's nice to know that the fund is able to remain consistent because it enables long-term investors to grow their income through additional contributions and reinvested dividends over time.

However, it's worth noting that most of the distributions have historically been funded by net investment income. When a distribution is funded by net investment income, the dividends are typically classified as ordinary income. Therefore, BST will have tax implications that investors must consider if held outside a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on BST as the latest annual report confirms the fund's strength. The NAV continues to trend upward as the fund out earns its distributions. Since the option strategy is only implemented against less than half of the assets, BST is uniquely able to generate income while also participating in bull markets of the technology sector. I am optimistic about the future of tech and AI, and I believe that BST can continue to provide superior income and capital appreciation over time. The distributions remain well-supported, and the consistent track record of dividends means that long-term investors can efficiently compound their income by reinvesting distributions or adding new capital.

data.title: MercadoLibre: Competitive Landscape And Q1 Results
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying MercadoLibre (NASDAQ:MELI) shares. This article is a continuation of my initial coverage article published in SA on April 10, 2024, with the same recommendation.

In this article, I will tell investors who have not bought the shares that a compelling opportunity is emerging. Shopee, a division of Sea Limited (NYSE:SE), has been dominating the spotlight and growing a lot.

Many investors are worried about a possible threat to MercadoLibre's dominant position in Brazil, but I do not share this concern. Additionally, we will talk about the good results that the company reported in 1Q25.

Corporate Profile

MercadoLibre is a global e-commerce company founded in 1999 in Argentina. The company currently operates in 18 countries, with the three main contributors being Brazil, Argentina, and Mexico.

In addition to its e-commerce operations, MercadoLibre also has a digital banking operation, known as MercadoPago. At the end of 2024, the e-commerce operation accounted for 59% of total revenues, and the fintech operation accounted for 41%.

Revisiting The Thesis

On April 10, 2024, I published the article in which I first covered MercadoLibre, and since then, the stock has delivered a good return to investors.

At the time, I compared the company to peers such as Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Shopee. In this article, I intend to delve deeper into this competitive dynamic, especially in the Brazilian market. But why Brazil? Because the country is the most relevant for the company, as we can see in the revenue segmentation below.

Shopee's Growth

It's not uncommon to see news about Shopee, Sea Limited's e-commerce segment, expanding and opening new distribution centers in Brazil. But what does this mean for the Brazilian e-commerce sector? Data indicates that MercadoLibre is still the leader in market share in Brazil, however, Shopee is already in second place, even surpassing Amazon.

A recent article by Itaú BBA, a segment of Itaú (NYSE:ITUB), brought relevant information about the sector. Shopee has consolidated itself as the second-largest player in Brazil, with a GMV (Gross Merchandise Value) of $10 billion, twice Amazon's GMV and 40% of MercadoLibre's. Estimates indicate that Shopee doubled its GMV in one year.

Given this aggressive growth, many investors fear that Shopee could threaten MercadoLibre's leadership and hegemony in Brazil. I do not believe in this thesis, and I will give my reasons in the next chapter.

Amazon Has Already Tried

We must remember that MercadoLibre began its operations in the 1990s, and the company entered Brazil in the 2000s. Among the largest players, MercadoLibre is more familiar with the economy and the habits of the Brazilian consumer.

Additionally, Amazon entered Brazil in 2012 in a conservative manner. But since then, the company has never managed to threaten MercadoLibre's position, and this is Amazon, one of the magnificent seven. Will Shopee be able to do what Amazon failed to do?

Analyzing The Strategy

Let's dive deeper into the details of the Itaú BBA article. Shopee's average price per item is $7, compared to MercadoLibre's $21. Additionally, the company is trying to increase this average ticket by selling higher-ticket products, such as home appliances. However, we must remember that Brazil deals with interest rates of almost 15% and high household debt. Will this strategy work?

Moreover, 90% of Shopee's GMV is generated by Brazilian sellers, and 33% of these sellers had never sold on any e-commerce site. Is the strategy of serving as a gateway for sellers interesting? I think so at the moment, but I don't see a long-term future here.

MercadoLibre Is Not Just Watching

The company is not just watching Shopee's growth. MercadoLibre recently announced a $6.4 billion investment in Brazil. This is the largest investment announced for the sector and represents an increase in MercadoLibre's investments in Brazil of 48% YoY.

Competition is nothing new for the company. We must remember that MercadoLibre surpassed all local Brazilian players in Brazil during the 2000s and 2010s, and not even Amazon was able to threaten its hegemony.

I don't believe that Shopee will succeed. I'm also not saying that I'm skeptical about Sea Limited's thesis, on the contrary, I just believe that the dominance of Brazilian e-commerce will remain in the hands of MercadoLibre. To enrich our analysis, let's look at the financial analysis of the companies.

Financial Analysis and Valuation

We can see below that the financial analysis is very positive for MercadoLibre, compared to its competitors. The company has the highest revenue growth in the last 5 years, the highest ROE, and has high margins.

Financial metrics come at a price, and the company has the highest PEG among its competitors, but here, I propose a different perspective. Therefore, we will analyze the PEG from the company's own history.

The company is currently trading at the lowest PEG in the last 12 months. If we consider that the PEG of 1.67x returns to the average of 2.3x, we have an upside of 37% for the thesis, which corroborates the buy recommendation.

Latest Earnings Results

MercadoLibre released its 1Q25 results on May 7, and they were very good. As a reference, the company reported net revenue of $5.9 billion (estimate was $5.53 billion) and net income of $494 million (LSEG consensus estimate was $420.9 million).

In the financial segment, MercadoPago's credit portfolio grew 75% year-on-year, and there was a 31% YoY increase in the number of monthly active users, reaching 64 million customers.

In the Commerce segment, Brazil represents about 50% of revenues, and sales in the Brazilian marketplace increased 30% y/y to $5.3 billion. This shows the dominance of the company, and raises the competitive bar for Shopee.

But the big highlight did not come from Brazil, but from Argentina. Argentina was the biggest highlight, with GMV growth of 126% on a neutral exchange rate basis. This Argentine impulse materializes my thesis of April 10, 2024, where I stated that President Milei's policies could make the Argentine operation one of the vectors of growth in the result.

Moreover, the number of items sold increased by 28%, to 492 million, while the total number of unique buyers reached around 67 million. This was the quarter with the highest growth in new users since 2021.

In my opinion, the result was excellent, and the shares rose more than 6% in the post-market. As for the outlook, the company confirms its dominance and shifts all the pressure on its competitors. It is incredible how the company manages to grow so much even in adverse scenarios.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

To talk about risks, we need to talk a little about the latest earnings. Investments and growth in the credit segment have had an effect on profitability as the company deals with competition.

As we can see above, the gross margin did not grow, and the growth of only 8.3% y/y in net income does not seem to justify MercadoLibre's high valuation.

The high valuation level can be confirmed by the F valuation rating below. Additionally, it is important to note that SA's powerful Quant tools recommend that investors hold their shares, which differs from my buy recommendation.

The Bottom Line

The aggressive growth of Shopee, a segment of Sea Limited, in the Brazilian market has caught the attention of investors worldwide. Some believe that the company will soon overtake MercadoLibre, I disagree.

MercadoLibre is accustomed to competition, the company has long known the habits of Brazilian consumers. It is no wonder that not even Amazon has been able to threaten the company's hegemony in Brazilian e-commerce.

The results released in Q1 were very good, showing the strength of the Brazilian Commerce operation and the potential that the Argentine operations have to add to the business. The outlook is very positive.

Based on this analysis, I recommend buying MercadoLibre shares. In my view, this entire competitive context has turned into an extra discount for MercadoLibre shares, which makes me optimistic about the thesis.

data.title: Fidelity Corporate Bond Fund Q1 2025 Review
data.description:
Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Corporate Bond Fund is a credit-focused bond strategy that seeks a high level of current income.
  • Benchmarked against the Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index, the fund seeks to deliver competitive risk-adjusted performance commensurate with investor expectations of a primarily investment-grade corporate bond fund.
  • Utilizing a team-based investment process, the fund relies on experienced portfolio managers, research analysts and traders. We concentrate on areas where we believe we can repeatedly add value, including security selection, industry allocation and opportunistic trading.
  • Robust governance and risk management – consisting of extensive quantitative modeling, formal and informal portfolio reviews, and proprietary tools – support the identification of both opportunities and risks.

1LifeofFund(LOF)ifperformanceislessthan10years.Fund inception date: 05/04/2010.

2This expense ratio is from the most recent prospectus and generally is based on amounts incurred during the most recent fiscal year, or estimated amounts for the current fiscal year in the case of a newly launched fund.Itdoesnotincludeanyfeewaiversorreimbursements,whichwouldbereflectedinthefund'snetexpense ratio.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate; therefore, you may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance stated. Performance shown is that of the fund's Retail Class shares (if multiclass). You may own another share class of the fund with a different expense structure and, thus, have different returns. To learn more or to obtain the most recent month-end or other share-class performance, visit fidelity.com/performance, institutional.fidelity.com, or 401k.com. Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Taxable Bond Market Review

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 2.78% in the first quarter of 2025, according to the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, as market uncertainty around the possibility of steep tariffs by the U.S. on its foreign trade partners weighed on domestic stocks and led some investors to seek haven in fixed-income assets, particularly U.S. Treasurys.

In the previous quarter, bond yields rose markedly, while prices, which move in the opposite direction of yields, retreated. Though the U.S. stock market responded favorably to the election outcome on November 5 – and to the prospect of potential tax cuts and deregulation under a second Trump administration – the bond market was concerned with the possibility that certain proposed policy changes might exacerbate already stubborn inflation. In mid-December 2024, the U.S. Federal Reserve acknowledged that the pace of the monetary easing it began in September would be dependent on further progress toward its inflation target. As of December 31, the nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yield stood at 4.58%, having climbed 77 basis points (0.77%) in Q4.

In contrast, the 10-year yield fell 35 bps in Q1, closing the quarter at 4.23%. Historically, U.S. Treasury yields tend to drop when economic uncertainty rises – and uncertainty rose sharply during the three months. Equity markets whipsawed as President Trump and members of the administration adopted alternately aggressive and soothing rhetoric about the President's plans. On March 4, the administration implemented tariffs on certain goods from Mexico and Canada, only to postpone them two days later. As of quarter end, the President had declared April 2 as "Liberation Day" – that is, the day that the full scale of his trade policy would be announced.

As stock and bond markets reacted, the overall trend was a reversal from the risk-on environment of Q4 to a risk-off mindset in Q1 of the new year. By month, the Aggregate index advanced a modest 0.53% in January, when U.S. stocks were still on the rise but a tame inflation report quelled rising bond yields; it then gained 2.20% in February, as movement by investors to de-risk out of equities and into fixed income gained steam; and was essentially flat (+0.04%) in March, as market participants awaited the April announcement on tariffs. The Fed, for its part, maintained its "wait and see" stance during the quarter, pausing further action on policy interest rates.

Against a backdrop of falling yields, fixed-income categories with greater rate sensitivity enjoyed the best price gains. Long-term (10+ year) debt issues advanced 3.40% and outpaced intermediate- and short-term issues, with returns weakest at the front end (1-3 years) of the curve (+1.63%). By credit rating, bonds rated AA had a notable performance advantage over highest-quality AAA bonds and lower-quality investment-grade securities rated A and BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB).

Results at the sector level reflected a flight to quality, with U.S. Treasurys (+2.92%) and the high-quality mortgage-backed securities segment (+3.06%) outperforming higher-risk categories such as U.S. investment-grade corporate bonds (+2.31%), asset-backed securities (+1.53%) and commercial mortgage-backed securities (+2.57%) on a total return basis.

Outside the Aggregate index, U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities gained a robust 4.17%, per Bloomberg, while U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+1.00%) and emerging-markets high-income securities (+1.60%) lagged amid declining yields and widening spreads for credit-based assets.

Source: Bloomberg *Over similar-duration Treasuries

Performance Review

DETAILED FUND ATTRIBUTION RELATIVE TO BENCHMARK

Strategy: Sector Allocation

Market Environment

All major fixed-income categories advanced in Q1, with U.S. Treasurys and mortgage-backed securities outperforming higher-risk segments such as U.S. investment-grade corporate bonds and asset-backed securities.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Sector allocation aided performance versus the benchmark Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index.

  • An out-of-benchmark stake in U.S. Treasurys – which the fund held, along with cash, for liquidity and hedging purposes – was the top contributor. (Positive)
  • Non-benchmark exposure to high-yield bonds also modestly helped. (Positive)
  • In contrast, an underweight to corporate bonds from the industrial sector, particularly within the technology segment, detracted. (Negative)

Strategy: Security Selection

Market Environment

Among maturities, long-term (10+ years) bonds fared best amid declining rates. By credit quality, securities rated AA had a notable performance advantage over other investment-grade bonds.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Security selection also contributed to the fund's relative performance.

  • Within the financials sector, bond selection among banks helped, led by UBS. (Positive)
  • In industrials, picks among pharmaceutical and health care companies, including Bayer and CVS, contributed as well. (Positive)
  • Also within industrials, however, results among issuers in the automotive group detracted. (Negative)

Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve

Market Environment

U.S. Treasury yields fell across the yield curve during the quarter, led by two- to 10-year maturities.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Yield-curve positioning – particularly in the intermediate-maturity portion of the curve – boosted relative performance this quarter.

  • The fund's duration, a measure of sensitivity to interest rates, was slightly longer than that of the benchmark during a period of declining bond yields. (Positive)

Outlook and Positioning

In our conversations with corporate management teams, we're hearing considerable caution in their approaches to capital expenditures, forward guidance, etc. Despite the spread widening that occurred late in the period, credit spreads remain tight compared with long-term historical averages. Given these factors, we likewise have a cautious outlook and are being conservative about adding risk to the portfolio. That said, in light of our risk- management-minded allocation to U.S. Treasurys and cash, we're in a position to capitalize on investment opportunities if volatility picks up and spreads widen.

Over the past year plus, corporate credit, and fixed-income securities in general, have enjoyed a tailwind from higher yields. With bond yields declining since mid-January against the backdrop of the Trump administration signaling its preference for lower interest rates across the curve, the tailwind of higher yields may dissipate. If the current uncertainty sparked by the administration's fiscal policy leads to slower economic growth, we believe yields could fall in that scenario as well. If that occurs, it could have a major influence on corporate spreads, in our view.

From a supply/demand standpoint, dynamics have been favorable for some time. There's been a lot of supply, which has been met by strong demand. Year to date, corporate issuance has remained robust, suggesting that the market is on track for another year of substantial new issuance.

In the near term, given the uncertainty surrounding the administration's policies regarding tariffs, government spending cuts and other fiscal matters, we think the Fed will remain in a "wait and see" mode concerning further policy interest rate cuts. That said, with nervousness about the odds of an economic slowdown on an upswing, investors are beginning to anticipate the possibility of three more cuts this year.

Within this context, we will continue to implement our investment strategy by emphasizing what we consider to be our research team's best ideas. We will focus on issuers that we believe have a durable credit profile with bonds that, in our view, offer attractive long-term risk/return dynamics.

We're planning to keep the fund's overall credit risk in check, while focusing on security selection as we try to outperform the benchmark. At the same time, we plan to continue having a meaningful liquidity cushion of U.S. Treasurys and cash in the portfolio, as we believe this "dry powder" will enable us to capitalize on compelling credit opportunities as they arise. As of March 31, roughly 7% of the fund's assets were allocated to Treasurys and about 2% were in cash.

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: VTEC: Difficult To Work Out Who This Is Suitable For
data.description:
Horses for courses

As I've been known to point out, much of economics - not all, but much - is simply the encoding of folk wisdom. Possibly made a little precise so that it can be tested, definitely put into more obscure and obfuscating language so that it can be charged for.

Some of that is also true about finance and financials, as here. The obvious point we have to grasp is that horses for courses. There are varied instruments out there, each with a different design. That design dictates how they work, fairly obviously. But that also means that we need to grasp those basics so that we use them for the appropriate purpose.

If we don't grasp the design, then we'll quite possibly use the instrument to try to do something it just won't. That would not be good for whatever it was that we were trying to achieve by that use, obviously. But it also opens us up to the possibility of losing substantial sums of our hard-earned money, which is rather worse.

Just as an example, and to be extreme, playing in futures and options is not the way to gain a risk free and consistent stream of income. Nor are Treasury bills likely to provide us with a large capital gain. On the other hand, Treasuries and consistent and risk free incomes are perfectly consonant, as are large capital gains - and losses - in options and futures.

Municipal bonds

Munis - municipal - are the third grand class of bonds in the US markets. Treasuries - The Feds - corporates and munis. Treasuries are "risk free," which they aren't. But they are credit risk free - which they're not really either, for governments can and will go bust. It's just that if the Feds go bust, we've all got much greater problems than the value of any financial savings we might have once had. So we consider them credit risk free concentrating upon inflation and interest rate risk.

Corporate bonds, obviously, companies can go bust. So can municipalities. So, in this sense, corporate and munis belong together. The implication of their credit risk is that if we decide we want to play in those markets, then we need to be diversified.

One problem with this is that bond markets are pretty thin. Not much trading of secondary bonds out there - the average bond, so I'm told, trades twice, at issue and redemption. Spreads on smaller issues can be pretty wide. We can indeed build a diversified portfolio, but it'll be by buying at issue and holding until that redemption. Just an example, but large areas of the European corporate bond market operate in $200k minimum par lot sizes. That's a bit chunky for near all private or retail investors like us.

So, the method of gaining that diversification across credit risks it to buy into a fund. Could be an ETF, or a closed end fund, there are other options as well. An open-ended fund will rarely trade far off net asset value, a closed end fund can wander quite a long way from it. On the other hand, a closed end fund cannot be subject to a run in the same way an open-ended can.

The problem with open-ended funds is the potential liquidity of the underlying. If lots of people wish to redeem, then the fund must sell, obviously. But if the underlying they're trying to sell is illiquid, then they'll have difficulty doing so, or must do so at distress prices. This increases the incentive to cash out, raises redemptions, and it becomes a run.

In something liquid like Treasuries or major stocks, this isn't a problem. In small bond issues, private companies, or real estate, it can be. Several funds over the years in those last two have fallen over as a result of this problem.

So, if we want to be in munis we really want to be in a fund of some kind. Unless we're very much larger than the average investor, that's really the way we'll gain the diversification we need in the face of credit risk.

Munis and tax

The big issue with municipal bonds is that they're usually (not always!) free of the Federal income tax. Further, instate issuers are usually free of the state income tax for instate holders of those bonds. NAN is just such a fund aimed at New York State residents, for example.

The effect of this is as an economist would predict. Munis pay lower interest rates (more accurately, coupons) than their credit risk a time tenor would usually indicate. Lower than Treasuries, even. We can see the effect of the tax exemption here:

That's the average across all issues. It's also the effect of taxes on the interest rates munis have to pay. If the interest is taxable, then they pay 1.6% more than if it isn't. Logically, investors are buying taking account of tenor, credit risk and so on, and so the tax exemption is worth that much to them.

Yes, true, economists claim a little too much that markets clear and so on, but here they're about right. The after tax return to munis that aren't taxed should be, given consistent risk and time span, the same as taxable ones. And more detailed research tends to find that this is true, the coupons on munis are pushed down by the tax benefits received by those who buy them.

Vanguard California Tax-Exempt Bond ETF (BATS:VTEC)

So, funds which hold tax-exempt bonds - what we call munis most of the time - make sense for those who pay tax. They make more sense for those who pay higher rates of tax - recall, above, the coupon is determined by the *average* tax bill of buyers, so if you're a higher than average taxpayer you get a greater than market benefit. We want a fund because diversification of credit risk.

In states where there's a high income tax chargeable at that state level, it might well be worth looking into ways to alleviate that burden, as well as the Federal one. So, here we are, a state-based muni bond fund. Carrying CA issues and clearly designed for CA residents. Because munis are only free of state income tax instate and for instate taxpayers.

So, in considering VTEC, if you're not a California resident, then this is of no interest to you. If you're not a Federal taxpayer, this is also of no interest to you. For income non-taxpayers in California, this is just a horse not for your course.

Tax equivalent yield

The standard way of calculating this all out is to look at these tax-privileged investments (and we can do it with any tax-privilege we like) and their returns, then work out what you'd have to get as a non-tax-privileged yield to match it. The tax system is complicated, many different rates and all that, but that's the core of it. Fortunately, people publish tables of this stuff, like this one for CA residents:

As we can see, the tax break is worth more and more to the investor as income - and so marginal tax rate - rises. A 5% yield is really worth 10.9% to someone at the very tippy top of the income/tax range. In the sense that the cash in hand gained from a 5% untaxable is the same as a 10.9% taxed.

Now, note something interesting about this. At low tax-free yields the tax-free investment isn't worth it - because taking the taxable yield, paying taxes, and grumbling about it produces more cash in hand. And this is where, as far as I'm concerned, VTEC has a problem.

VTEC

There's this:

That yield there is 2.9%. OK, so running that up to the tax equivalent yield - note the already included CA income tax - means that we need to find a taxable yield of 4.5% if we're in the 100k to 200k income range or 6.5% if we're over 1 million already.

Well, OK, we can get 4.77% in short-term Treasuries - so no credit nor interest rate risk - in USFR. Or even 6.2% in MQY, which is already free of Federal income tax, even if the CA one still has to be paid.

The whole and entire point - what makes them attractive - of muni bonds is that the tax-free yield equals or beats what we can get in taxable yields and then pay tax upon. A muni bond fund that doesn't achieve that isn't, to my mind, working at the task it's been set to do.

We can beat VTEC's income by being in less risky (so Treasuries) or equally risky (a national muni fund). So, VTEC doesn't work.

The usual financial details

And then fund facts:

And fund holdings:

Please note that I'm not saying there's something wrong here. Vanguard is charging very little for this (0.08%) but that's where I think the problem actually lies.

This isn't an "index fund" even though it is, in fact, benchmarked against an index of CA muni bonds. Not in the sense that we think of the indices we actually know, S&P 500 and so on. It doesn't even hold all the issues which do make up the index is it benchmarked against.

On the other hand, it is, sorta, an index fund in that it holds a vast swathe of positions and does not select (at least not very much) between them. It's deliberately holding - and this is why the fee is 0.08% - "the market" and not being selective.

Why I'm wrong

Now, of course, there could be something I've missed here. But I've looked through and seen that the same general risk (so, medium term munis) funds offer great cash in hand return. Even with the CA tax to pay. For everyone except the top tax rate payers, even short-term Treasuries look similar in end-return.

I agree, because I always do, that I could be wrong. If I am doing, tell me as well - that's how I learn.

But I'm not wrong in the yield on this fund, nor that better is available elsewhere at what seems to be similar risk. This is true even if we move from published yield to SEC 30 day and so on.

My opinion

Municipal bonds are Federal income tax free. Instate ones are, to instate taxpayers, state income tax free. Therefore, in states with high income taxes, there are muni bond funds that cater to that market.

But the entire point is to end up with a better final return than investing for yield and then paying the taxes. This is a test that - as far as I can see - VTEC fails. Until about halfway up the income distribution, it's possible to do better than VTEC by being in simply floating rate Treasuries (USFR). And it's possible to better in a managed muni - rather than index - national fund and then pay state taxes (MQY).

It's possible that at the very tippy top of the tax and income distribution this changes, but I'm still not seeing it.

I think the reason for this is that VTEC holds "the market" rather than optimising for the income through managed holdings.

As I said at the top, the design of an instrument is important, this tells us who it is good for, what purpose it is good for, what problem it solves. I'm not seeing that VTEC successfully solves the problem.

Do something else.

Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

International equities rose in the first quarter. Financial markets were rattled by seismic shifts in US foreign policy, which significantly altered the outlook for global trade, economic growth, and inflation. Enthusiasm for AI-related expansion and a more accommodative US business environment waned in response to rising economic and tariff uncertainty. Consequently, capital rotated away from the US to international markets, particularly Europe, which significantly outperformed amid signs of a more favorable economic backdrop, including less restrictive monetary policy, improving leading indicators such as manufacturing output, and greater fiscal stimulus.

Performance Summary

Hartford International Equity Fund (I Share) (6.01%) outperformed the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index (Net) (5.23%) during the quarter.

Security selection drove relative outperformance, as strong selection in the financials and industrials sectors was partially offset by weaker selection in the consumer discretionary and communication services sectors.

Sector allocation, a result of the portfolio’s bottom-up stock-selection process, detracted from relative results. The negative effects from our overweight exposure to information technology and underweight exposure to financials were partially offset by the positive impact from our overweight exposure to communication services.

Factor impact on the Fund was negative, driven by its smaller cap footprint and underweight to stocks with more financial leverage.

From a top-down perspective, regional exposures (country and currency) were relatively flat, as the negative impacts from the Fund’s positioning in Europe and Emerging Markets were offset by exposure to names in Japan, which contributed.

The top relative contributors to performance during the quarter included our positions in Rheinmetall (industrials) and Prudential (financials).

Positioning & Outlook

Increased volatility and signs of a shift away from US exceptionalism defined equity markets as investors navigated the impact of new US tariffs and the Trump administration's uncertain agenda. Consequently, this turbulence resulted in a breadth of international opportunities as the US momentum trade reversed and foreign economies experienced various tailwinds, including increased fiscal spending and quantitative easing. We believe this increased market volatility and potential global regime shift sets up fundamental stock pickers for success as they differentiate between stocks that can navigate these geopolitical uncertainties and those that cannot. As ever, we remain focused on balancing risks in the portfolio and structurally allocate across different managers and styles to provide diversified opportunities in various market areas.

At the end of the period, our largest overweights were to the communication services and information technology sectors, while our largest underweights were to the financials and utilities sectors, relative to the benchmark.

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the prospects of individual companies. • Foreign investments may be more volatile and less liquid than U.S. investments and are subject to the risk of currency fluctuations and adverse political, economic and regulatory developments. These risks may be greater, and include additional risks, for investments in emerging markets or if the Fund focuses in a particular geographic region or country. • Small- and mid-cap securities can have greater risks and volatility than large-cap securities. • The securities of large market capitalization companies may underperform other segments of the market. • The Fund's strategy for allocating assets among portfolio management teams may not work as intended. • The value of securities selected using quantitative analysis can perform differently from the market as a whole or from their expected performance.

Investors should carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in a fund's full prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by visiting hartfordfunds.com. Please read it carefully before investing.

data.title: NatWest Group: A Double Beat To Start The Year
data.description:
For all the uncertainty out there, 2025 is still shaping up to be a good year for NatWest Group (NYSE:NWG). On the business front, first quarter results could hardly have gone better, with the bank cruising past consensus on the way to an exceptional 18.5% return on tangible equity. As for the stock, well, things have gone just as well. Aided by a weaker U.S. dollar, NatWest's ADSs have returned another ~20% since my last update in February, bringing their year-to-date return up to the 30% mark.

This strong run leaves the stock trading for almost 1.4x tangible book value. In past years, I would have considered that nosebleed territory for a European bank. Today? I am not so sure, with NatWest still enjoying a multi-year tailwind from hedge income. While the potential for multiple expansion is more limited from here, the strength of NatWest's earnings still justifies its current valuation. Overall, I remain bullish.

A Double Beat To Start The Year

NatWest reported its first quarter financials last week. Results were about as good as they could have been, with the bank comfortably beating consensus on both revenue and earnings.

As my regular readers will know, NatWest's interest rate hedging program has been the key component of my bullish case. Swaps written when interest rates were very low continue to mature, and the associated pick-up in yield is powering both margin expansion and growth in net interest income. Being a hedge, this trend is running counter-cyclical to wider U.K. interest rates now that the Bank of England is in easing mode.

This continues to play out quite nicely. Last quarter, NatWest reported total income of £3.98 billion, beating consensus by around 4%. Within that, net interest income was £3.03 billion, beating by roughly £40 million. Earning asset balances were basically in line, but the bank's margin came in better than expected. NatWest's net interest margin was 2.27% last quarter, up 22bps year-on-year, 8bps quarter-on-quarter and around 4bps ahead of consensus. Per below, hedge dynamics are set to be a major tailwind here over the next couple of years, and this will power further gains in NIM and NII.

Non-interest income was also strong this quarter, rising by nearly 16% year-on-year to £954 million. The bank's markets business was particularly buoyant, with elevated volatility contributing to strong growth in trading income, while underwriting income from capital markets was also solid. Following this performance, management tightened full-year revenue guidance toward the high-end of its previous range (£15.2-£15.7 billion). Given trade tensions and the threat to economic growth, I could see potential for weakness in certain areas like capital markets, but that shouldn't matter much: the strength of last quarter's revenue print combined with ongoing hedge tailwinds make £15.7 billion comfortably doable, if not outright conservative.

Turning to costs, operating expenses of £1.98 billion beat by around 6%. Credit charges came in slightly weaker, missing by over 10%, but this is a difficult line to forecast accurately quarter-to-quarter. More importantly, asset quality continues to look solid, with Stage 3 (i.e. non-performing) loan balances ticking up just 10bps quarter-on-quarter relative to total loans. At 1.5%, this remains around 20bps below NatWest's recent historical average. Balances of early-stage delinquencies (loans less than 30-days past due) also remain flat.

Of course, most of the recent tariff-related volatility only came in the current quarter. We will only begin to see the full impact in later periods. With that in mind, NatWest looks prudently provisioned. Its current expected credit loss allowance currently stands at around £3.5 billion, and this includes around £300 million in post-model overlays. Under its Base Case scenario, the bank would actually release a modest number of provisions. While it would need to raise more in a more severe downturn, it is also earning around £7.5 billion in annual pre-provision income to absorb losses. Accordingly, credit isn't a major worry of mine.

Weighing all that up, NatWest reported an attributable profit of £1.25 billion last quarter. That was around 18% ahead of consensus, with the associated return on tangible equity (18.5%) beating by 270bps.

The Valuation

As management noted on the call, there was some lumpiness around costs and non-interest income that basically went the bank's way last quarter. That means we shouldn't be too quick to extrapolate this performance over the rest of the year.

Having said that, let's place the bank's earnings into some context. The shares trade for £4.76 each as I type ($12.85 per ADS), equivalent to 1.37x NatWest's tangible net asset value ('TNAV') at the end of last quarter. An 18.5% return on tangible equity would equate to a price-earnings ratio of just 7.4x. As you can see, shareholders can afford to see quite a bit of slippage before these shares trade for over 10x earnings.

The sell-side analysts that cover this bank don't expect that to happen. Consensus has ROTE landing in the 16%-17% area throughout the 2025-2027 period, which would keep the stock's P/E multiple in the single-digit area.

That basically explains why I remain so bullish here. Furthermore, because NatWest's earnings are so strong, shareholders are seeing strong growth in TNAV per share while also receiving a nice dividend. For example, last quarter's TNAV of £3.47 per share represented year-on-year growth of roughly 15%. NatWest also paid out £0.215 per share in cash dividends in that time, equal to a 4.5% trailing yield.

There's another element powering the bank's balance sheet growth: the unwind of the cash flow hedge reserve. Just as U.S. banks have seen unrealized losses on bonds depress their book values, the same is true of NatWest's interest rate swaps. This will fade as the hedge continues to be repriced over the next couple of years, contributing to TNAV growth. Between balance sheet growth, a flat TNAV multiple and dividends, these shares continue to offer today's buyer the potential for double-digit annualized returns.

Conclusion

NatWest begins its fiscal 2025 in great shape, with first quarter results comfortably exceeding expectations. Lumpiness around costs and non-interest income mean that last quarter's 18.5% ROTE is probably a high-water mark rather than a base, but the valuation hardly requires the bank to maintain that level of profitability. Plus, growing hedge income is going to keep its earnings at a very healthy level regardless. Since this is likely to keep the stock's P/E ratio in the single-digit region, I remain bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PepsiCo Poised For Market-Beating Returns
data.description:
PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) (NEOE:PEP:CA) is a global brand powerhouse with a storied history. Overt time, the business's economics have proven incredibly appealing, providing investors with attractive returns for a long period of time. However, the company's stock has run into some headwinds of late, and there are a number of explanations out there purportedly accounting for PEP's difficulties. Ultimately, however, I think these explanations are misleading investors. On the contrary, I think that PEP is currently trading at significantly attractive prices (~$131 at the time of writing), which should provide good returns for investors going forward.

General Business Overview

Quite obviously, everyone has heard of Pepsi’s flagship carbonated soft drink, Pepsi Cola. However, newcomers to the Pepsi business are likely often surprised at how relatively small of a percentage the legacy cola business contributes to the overall enterprise. The company describes its business lines as follows:

In a recent investor presentation, they present their brand portfolio in this way:

Here is how sales across divisions stacked up in 2024:

And here are how operating profits across divisions fared during the same time:

Furthermore, these trends have persisted over time. Here are sales numbers over the past ten years for the 4 largest divisions:

… and here are operating profits over the same time frame:

So, with this by way of background, we can already note several key points that offer a more accurate summary of Pepsi’s business which would augment the likely inaccurate stereotype of the company someone completely new to the business might have:

  • The company’s sales are a roughly 60% - vs. - 40% split United States - vs. - Rest of the world
  • While the beverage business makes up a majority of revenues, it is actually the salty snacks and convenience foods business that drives profitability.
  • While certain brands (e.g., Pepsi and Lay’s) might make up an over-representation of sales, one should think of the company in terms of an extensive brand portfolio as opposed to simply two or three brands.

Current State of Pepsi’s Stock

So, with that, by way of background of the company, let’s fast-forward to present-day and see what’s going on with the stock. Here is the comparative chart for the total return of PEP vs. the S&P 500 over the past 10 years:

PEP has mostly performed in-line with the S&P500 over this period. Notably, though, performance over the past year or so has been particularly bad. I’ve seen a number of candidate explanations for this trend, some of which include the following:

  • A more health-conscious consumer that is opting out of Pepsi’s core brands
  • A greater trend in usage of GLP-1’s indirectly creating negative demand pressure
  • Product recalls, especially in the Frito-Lay and Quaker Foods divisions
  • RFK Jr.'s influence as head of the Department of Health and Human Services (HSS) and the influences policies he is spearheading would have on companies like Pepsi
  • Future expectations regarding further margin pressures from tariffs and increased supply chain costs

And this is not an exhaustive list, although it largely accounts for the general vibes that seem to be out there. In my opinion, though, this situation calls to mind the old adage that “news follows the stock price” – i.e., if you need an explanation for a stock’s price going up, you can find one, and if you need an explanation for a stock’s price going down, you can find one as well.

In any case, for dividend aficionados out there, and for value investors more generally, this state of affairs has made Pepsi's stock begin to look pretty attractive. For anyone who doesn’t know, Pepsi is a dividend king – i.e., a stock which has increased its dividend for the last 50 years+.

And with the stock having come down so much of late, new investors can buy in at a historically low starting dividend yield – 4.11% at the time of writing.

My Assessment

So, what do I think of the current opportunity with Pepsi’s stock? Well, first of all, as I’ve already mentioned, I’m very suspicious of the stereotypical explanations that are out there. However, beyond simply hand-waving, let’s engage some of these more in-depth.

RFK Jr. at HHS

Regarding this narrative, this chart is especially telling:

I’ve drawn a red-arrow pointing to the day that RFK Jr. was picked by Trump to head HHS. The stock price closed on that day (11/14/24) at $165.15 and hasn’t regained that level since. Thus, on its face, this would lend credence to the idea that RFK Jr. (and his preferred policies) is having an out-sized influence on the prospects (in investors' minds, at least) of a company like Pepsi.

However, to this, I would say that this is very welcome news to new Pepsi investors, if, in fact, RFK Jr.'s ascendancy at HHS is having the sort of impact on PEP's stock price as the above chart might suggest. I think that history has borne out the idea that, generally speaking, political figures (especially American political figures) turn out to be a lot less efficacious than people imagine they will be beforehand. Let's game out a few possibilities.

First, RFK Jr. is very much against artificial food dyes. The science on this, however, is highly controversial, as high-lighted here. This makes it more likely that litigation by companies like Pepsi might be able to stymy such regulatory efforts, if not fully derail them altogether. What's more, though, Pepsi has already vowed to phase out artificial dyes from many of its products by year-end, in any case, and, to my mind, it's not at all clear that doing so is going to massively affect the over-arching economics of their business.

Secondly, as discussed here, RFK Jr. is very much against high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS), and there has been discussion that he might seek to remove government subsidies that lower the price of HFCS. However, as the just-referenced article notes, it's important to note that Congress has the ultimate say over those subsidies, not the director of HHS. And, to my mind, it's unlikely that a Republican-controlled congress has sufficient interest in passing subsidies that harm corn farmers who overwhelmingly vote for Republicans.

Finally, RFK's general stance against highly processed foods is something that Republicans have historically balked at. Thus, it's not obvious to me that this sort of agenda will be sufficiently popular with the Trump base such that it can be enacted in a way meaningfully comparable to the promises which have been made.

Thus, my prediction would be that, while RFK Jr.’s agenda might present some headwinds for Pepsi, these are likely to be more short-term than long-term in nature, and, probably more importantly, the magnitude of their impact is likely to be less than the generally accepted current narrative would have you believe.

GLP-1’s and General Trend toward Greater Health Consciousness …

While there has definitely been a significant uptrend in GLP-1 usage over the last few years, it’s important to remember the scale we’re talking about here. According to this source, only 12% of adults have taken one of these drugs, and only 6% are currently taking them. Furthermore, about 6 out of 10 users of these drugs are taking them primarily for a condition other than weight loss (e.g., heart disease or diabetes), with roughly 4 out of 10 having said weight loss is their primary reason for use. Furthermore, as that article notes, Medicare does not currently cover drugs used for weight loss. This underscores a larger point – namely, that the prohibitively high-cost of these GLP-1’s is preventing their widespread adoption, and presumably their adoption over long periods of time, at least for the foreseeable future. All this to say that, while GLP-1’s might present a headwind at the margins, to my mind, there isn’t anything in the data suggesting they are going to be an existential headwind that is going to cut Pepsi’s sales in half or something of the like.

Furthermore, regarding general trends towards health consciousness, such an explanation would predict that we would see changes in Pepsi’s volumes. However, when we look at the data, it is not so clear. Here are the revenue data for PBNA and FLNA regarding the relative contributions of volume and pricing to total revenue growth over the past 10 years:

First, while the beverages segment has seen declines, prior to 2021, these declines, while persistent, had not been outstanding. Moreover, there was a sharp influx in volumes in 2021. Finally, since 2021, the idea that volume declines were the result of Pepsi’s out-sized pricing increases seems just as plausible of an explanation as that there has been some heightened trend of late (e.g., compared to prior to 2021) towards greater health consciousness.

Additionally, and more importantly for the company, since FLNA drives their profitability, there has been very little in the way of volume declines in the FLNA division (in fact, just the opposite up until 2021 before the influx of inflation). The declines that there have been have only really come in the last couple of years, and, as mentioned with the PBNA division, to my mind, these are just as likely the result of out-sized price increases as they were the result of some heightened trend towards healthier lifestyles.

Product recalls, tariffs, supply chain costs, etc.

When you have as many brands as Pepsi has and when you deal in the volume the company does, you’re likely to run into the need for recalls from time to time, unfortunately. Personally, I don’t see these as any more than a blip for the company that they will recover from in the long-term, thanks in large part to the trust and goodwill they’ve been able to engender in the public mind regarding their brands.

And, regarding tariffs, increased supply chain costs, etc., while these are a real thing for Pepsi’s business, they are a real thing for businesses generally. From a competitive perspective, as the previous discussion regarding volumes and pricing alluded to, Pepsi has been able to consistently raise prices year over year, and consumers have largely tolerated it. Sure, perhaps consumers have revolted over the past couple of years in response to some of Pepsi’s out-sized price hikes, but the company is likely simply learning where the limits of its pricing power stands. The point is, though, that the pricing power a company like Pepsi has is the envy of almost any company you could imagine – i.e., they’re about as well-positioned for headwinds like tariffs, supply-chain disruptions, etc., as you could reasonably ask for.

Valuation & Investor Takeaway

So where does this put us? Well, my thinking is that, if you’re at all sympathetic to the sort of counter-arguments I’ve offered here to the prevailing narratives that are out there regarding Pepsi, and if you have ever had any inclination to buy Pepsi’s stock, now is about as good of a time as any to buy. This is underscored by the starting dividend yield being at historically low levels, the company having the history and status of being a dividend king and all that entails (including its demonstrated ability to persist through the ebbs and flows of its sales volumes over time), and the wonderful economics the business enjoys primarily buoyed by the goodwill its brands generally have in consumers’ minds.

Here are a couple of back-of-the-napkin-style valuations that offer some insight into the kind of upside I think new investors are looking at, given current prices (again, ~$131/share at the time of writing).

First, assuming a 3% EPS growth rate and a 20x terminal multiple, I get a stock price of about $183 in 5 years. When you factor in dividend payments, this gets you close to a 50% total return. And I would underscore how conservative these assumptions are - a 20x terminal multiple is definitely on the low-end of where PEP has traded historically, and a 3% EPS growth rate is essentially keeping up with inflation, and, again, is assuming a dramatic drop-off from history for the company.

Second, on the optimistic side, if you grow EPS at about 6% (roughly in-line with historical trends) and assume a 25x terminal multiple (in the mid-to-higher-end of the historical range), I get a stock price of ~$264 in 5 years, for over a ~100% return, and this is to say nothing of the dividends collected along the way.

And really, based on any valuation method of your choosing, I think it's relatively easy to make the case that Pepsi’s stock could provide the sort of 50-100% total return numbers just mentioned over the next roughly 3–5 years. What's more, I think this means the company is well-positioned to out-perform the S&P 500 over this period. Thus, my recommendation is ‘buy.’
